pairs.question: What was Andrew McCabe's role at the FBI and when did he serve as acting director?
pairs.answer: Andrew McCabe served as the Deputy Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) from February 1, 2016 to January 29, 2018. He became the acting FBI Director on May 9, 2017, following the dismissal of James Comey by President Donald Trump.

pairs.question: Why did Andrew McCabe become acting director of the FBI?
pairs.answer: McCabe became acting FBI director because, in the absence of a Senate-confirmed Director, the Deputy Director automatically assumes the role of acting director, as per FBI regulations.

pairs.question: What were the reasons cited by Attorney General Jeff Sessions for firing Andrew McCabe?
pairs.answer: Attorney General Jeff Sessions cited reports from the Department of Justice (DOJ) Inspector General and the FBI's disciplinary office, stating that McCabe had made unauthorized releases of information to the media and had 'lacked candor' in discussions about it.

pairs.question: What legal consequences did Andrew McCabe face following his firing?
pairs.answer: On April 19, 2018, the DOJ Inspector General sent a criminal referral to federal prosecutors regarding possible criminal charges against McCabe for allegations of lying to internal investigators.

pairs.question: How did Andrew McCabe respond to his firing?
pairs.answer: McCabe denied ever being dishonest and stated that his firing was politically motivated. He argued that the allegations against him were unfounded and part of a broader political agenda.

pairs.question: When did Andrew McCabe return to the role of Deputy Director after his temporary acting directorship?
pairs.answer: McCabe resumed his position as Deputy Director on August 2, 2017, after Christopher A. Wray was sworn in as the new FBI Director.

pairs.question: What is the process by which a Deputy Director becomes acting FBI Director?
pairs.answer: If there is no Senate-confirmed Director, the Deputy Director automatically becomes the acting FBI Director. This ensures continuity in leadership during transitions or vacancies.
